entry.title: The tin racket: how illegal mining devastated Indonesia’s tin islands

link.title: The tin racket: how illegal mining devastated Indonesia’s tin islands

entry.content:
I reported this investigation with Shabrina at The Gecko Project, and coordinated a collaboration and joint publication with Follow The Money (Netherlands), Finnish public broadcaster Yle and KCIJ Newstapa (Korea).

The investigation

The Indonesian islands of Bangka and Belitung produce nearly a fifth of the world’s tin — the metal that solders together the circuit boards in virtually every electronic device. They have also been devastated by illegal mining: landscapes pockmarked with abandoned pits, seabeds churned by makeshift dredging pontoons, fisheries smothered in tailings.

The Tin Racket investigated how illegally mined tin moves from these operations into the global supply chain — reaching major technology companies including Apple, Samsung and Tesla.

Reporting on the ground in Bangka Belitung, we interviewed miners, middlemen and employees of PT Timah — the world’s fourth-largest tin producer and Indonesia’s state-owned giant — and analysed documents from one of the largest corruption trials in Indonesian history. Key findings:

  • Between 2015 and 2022, PT Timah deliberately sourced illegally mined tin through intermediaries and shell companies — a scheme for which its former president director was sentenced to 20 years in prison and fined $29 million.
  • Despite the convictions, illegal tin was still entering Timah’s supply chain: middlemen we spoke to openly admitted they don’t verify where the ore they buy was mined.
  • The audit systems that tech companies rely on — under the Responsible Minerals Initiative — fail to detect the environmental damage and human rights problems in the supply chain, giving brands false assurance.

Impact

The investigation put independent reporting on the ground in a region where the gap between corporate “responsible sourcing” claims and reality is vast, and confronted the world’s biggest electronics brands and the audit industry with evidence that their due diligence systems weren’t working.

It also established a baseline for continuing scrutiny of the sector. The Gecko Project has since reported on Indonesia’s subsequent military-backed crackdown on illegal tin mining, which has seen around $420 million in assets seized and handed to PT Timah — the same company at the centre of the corruption scandal — raising questions about whether the response addresses the systemic problems the investigation exposed or simply consolidates state power.

Behind the story

This investigation paired courtroom evidence with shoe-leather reporting: the corruption trial provided a paper trail showing how laundering illegal tin worked from the inside, while fieldwork showed it was still happening. It was co-published in four countries — with Follow The Money (in English and Dutch), Yle in Finland and KCIJ Newstapa in South Korea — reflecting the global reach of the tin supply chain itself.
entry.summary: An investigation into illegal tin mining in Bangka Belitung, tracing illegally mined tin from devastated landscapes into the supply chains of the world's biggest tech companies.

entry.title: Chasing shadows: the anonymous companies destroying Indonesia’s rainforests

link.title: Chasing shadows: the anonymous companies destroying Indonesia’s rainforests

entry.content:
I worked on this series of investigations at The Gecko Project, reporting alongside partners including the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists (ICIJ), Bloomberg News and Agence France-Presse.

The investigation

In the late 2010s, most of the world’s biggest palm oil and pulpwood producers pledged to stop destroying rainforest. Yet deforestation in Indonesia continued — driven increasingly by obscure companies whose real owners were hidden behind layers of offshore secrecy. This series of investigations asked: who is really behind them?

The first major investigation, Chasing Shadows (2023, published as part of ICIJ’s Deforestation Inc project), examined First Resources, a Singapore-listed palm oil giant that pledged zero deforestation in 2015. Drawing on testimony from fourteen former employees, corporate registry records and satellite imagery analysis, it found evidence that the company was linked to a network of “shadow companies” that had cleared more rainforest than any other corporate group in Southeast Asia over the preceding five years — close to 100,000 hectares since 2008 — while First Resources denied ownership or control. Insiders described the official group and the shadow companies as “just one big group”, and the trail of control ran through a British Virgin Islands entity.

A second investigation, published with Bloomberg News in 2024, used insider testimony from nine former employees to link Royal Golden Eagle (RGE) — the pulp and paper conglomerate behind APRIL, another high-profile zero-deforestation pledge — to shadow companies that ranked among the worst deforesters in Indonesia, registered through Samoa and the British Virgin Islands.

A third instalment, Shadow Integration (2026, with Agence France-Presse), used shipment-level trade data, ship tracking and satellite imagery to trace wood from those deforesting plantations through a new pulp mill and into RGE’s own supply chain — showing how the products of deforestation were being laundered into “sustainable” and even certified commodity flows reaching global consumer brands.

Impact

The series produced concrete commercial consequences. Following Chasing Shadows, eight consumer goods and chemicals companies — including Procter & Gamble, Danone, Henkel, BASF and Beiersdorf — suspended sourcing from First Resources, a development also covered by ICIJ. After Shadow Integration, consumer healthcare company Haleon ended purchases from RGE’s Chinese subsidiary Asia Symbol.

The investigations also fed into the international policy debate on corporate transparency. Our analysis of how secrecy jurisdictions facilitate deforestation documented how the worst deforesters in Indonesia were owned through the British Virgin Islands and Samoa. British MPs subsequently cited the “disturbing pattern” of BVI-owned companies degrading rainforests in pressing the UK government to force its overseas territories to open up beneficial ownership registers.

Behind the story

This series shows how environmental investigations can combine very different kinds of evidence: corporate registry filings across multiple secrecy jurisdictions, satellite deforestation analysis (with TheTreeMap), shipment-level trade data and ship tracking, and patient cultivation of insider sources — fourteen former employees in the First Resources investigation, nine in the RGE story.

It was also a genuinely cross-border collaboration. Chasing Shadows was co-published with ICIJ, El País, Süddeutsche Zeitung, Tempo, Follow The Money, De Tijd and WDR/NDR as part of Deforestation Inc; later instalments were produced with Bloomberg News and Agence France-Presse — taking findings about anonymously owned plantation companies in Borneo to audiences worldwide.
entry.summary: A series of investigations exposing how major conglomerates allegedly used anonymously owned "shadow companies" to clear rainforest while publicly pledging zero deforestation.

entry.title: The promise was a lie: how Indonesian villagers lost their cut of the palm oil boom

link.title: The promise was a lie: how Indonesian villagers lost their cut of the palm oil boom

entry.content:
I worked on this investigation at The Gecko Project, reporting in partnership with BBC News and Mongabay.

The investigation

Under a scheme known as “plasma”, palm oil companies in Indonesia have been legally required since 2007 to share a portion of their plantations — or the profits from them — with the communities whose land they occupy. This investigation set out to answer a simple question: were they actually doing it?

Over three years, our team analysed allegations against 155 plantation companies, examined 52 cases in depth, and interviewed more than 200 people — villagers, government officials, academics and company employees — across three of Indonesia’s largest islands. We combined fieldwork in dozens of villages with analysis of court records, company reports and government datasets.

The findings were stark:

  • Companies had failed to provide around 375,000 hectares of legally required plasma — an area three times the size of Los Angeles.
  • In Central Kalimantan province alone, villagers could be losing more than $90 million every year; researchers estimated communities could ultimately be deprived of some $8 billion.
  • Where plasma was provided, it often failed to deliver. A second instalment, Promised prosperity, drowning in debt, showed villagers locked into opaque 25-year contracts, earning less than $1 a day while their cooperatives’ debts to the companies ballooned — and traced palm oil from these schemes into the supply chains of Nestlé, Unilever and Kellogg’s.

Read the main story: The promise was a lie.

Impact

The investigation triggered an unusually rapid government response. Within days of publication, Indonesia’s chief economics minister announced an audit of the palm oil industry’s partnerships with smallholders, and Central Kalimantan’s governor established a task force on plasma. At least a dozen government actions followed, including parliamentary investigations and threats to revoke licences.

In 2023, the resulting audit vindicated our findings: the government found that only 581 of 2,864 companies audited — around 20% — were complying with their plasma obligations, and a task force spanning 14 ministries was set up in response.

The reporting also prompted action beyond government: six consumer goods companies privately reported steps to verify their suppliers’ compliance, and a petition calling on Mondelēz to stop buying from implicated companies gathered more than 129,000 signatures. BBC World aired a 16-minute segment and a 30-minute documentary based on the investigation.

The investigation won the 2023 TRACE Prize for Investigative Reporting and a Society of Publishers in Asia (SOPA) award for excellence in Bahasa Indonesia news reporting, and was named among the Global Investigative Journalism Network’s best investigative stories from Southeast Asia in 2022. Mongabay has published a full account of the investigation’s impact.

Behind the story

A distinctive feature of this investigation was its data-driven approach. We built the first database of conflicts driven by Indonesia’s plasma scheme, compiling more than 200 cases from local news reports, NGO documentation and government sources spanning a decade — then used it to identify patterns and target our field reporting. We released the dataset publicly under a Creative Commons licence, and published a methodological explainer so others could build on the work.

The investigation also prompted wider debate about corporate impunity in Indonesia’s plantation zone, including an essay by anthropologist Tania Li, published in our series, examining how state-corporate collusion allows companies to disregard the law.
entry.summary: An award-winning investigation with BBC News and Mongabay revealing how palm oil companies deprived Indonesian communities of billions of dollars in profits they were legally owed.
